site.name: Third Eye Worldwide
site.title: Third Eye Worldwide — Technology that opens new worlds

statsBand.label: Live in low- and middle-income countries
Price we charge for any of our tools

home.coreValuesSub: Every tool we ship, every partnership we sign, and every hire we make is measured against these. They are non-negotiable.

coreValues.title: Dignity first
coreValues.desc: Accessibility is not a feature we add at the end — it is the foundation we start from. Every user deserves full, independent access with no compromise.

coreValues.title: Nothing about us, without us
coreValues.desc: Half of our staff and leadership are visually impaired. No product, policy, or program is designed without the people it serves in the room.

coreValues.title: Open by default
coreValues.desc: All our core software is MIT-licensed. Translations are crowd-sourced. Knowledge and access should never be gatekept by cost, language, or geography.

coreValues.title: Built for where people live
coreValues.desc: We design for 2G networks, five-year-old smartphones, and the last mile. If it only works in a lab in San Francisco, it doesn't work.

coreValues.title: Radical transparency
coreValues.desc: Every dollar donated is tracked to a program. Every impact number is auditable. Every mistake we make is published — because trust is earned, not claimed.

features.title: Screen Reader
features.desc: Real-time audio descriptions and voice navigation for any web or mobile interface — in 40+ languages.

features.title: Visual Magnifier
features.desc: Dynamic screen magnification with edge detection and color enhancement for low-vision users.

features.title: Navigation Aid
features.desc: Audio-described wayfinding that guides users through cities, transit systems, and indoor spaces.

features.title: Digital Literacy
features.desc: Training workshops that teach visually impaired individuals to use technology confidently and safely.

features.title: Community Access
features.desc: Local partnerships that distribute devices and data plans to underserved communities in 47 countries.

features.title: Open Source
features.desc: All core tools are MIT licensed. Developers worldwide contribute fixes, translations, and features.

programs.heroSub: From assistive software to grassroots training, every program is designed for real-world independence. Every tool is free at point of use.

items.title: TEWW Screen Reader
items.desc: Our flagship open-source screen reader supports all major platforms and 40+ languages. Designed from the ground up by and for visually impaired users.

items.title: Visual Magnifier Pro
items.desc: Adaptive screen magnification with AI-enhanced edge detection, custom contrast modes, and cursor tracking for low-vision users.

items.title: Navigation Aid
items.desc: GPS and indoor positioning with real-time audio guidance. Partners with public transit systems in 12 cities to provide blind-friendly route data.

items.title: Digital Access Training
items.desc: Hands-on 4-week bootcamp teaching screen reader use, smartphone navigation, and online safety to newly visually impaired adults.

items.title: Device Lending Library
items.desc: Provides smartphones pre-configured with our tools to users in low-income settings. 18,000+ devices distributed since 2019.

items.title: Open Source Grants
items.desc: Annual grants for developers building accessible tools. Funded 63 projects across 28 countries, reaching 400K+ beneficiaries.

items.title: Audiolibrary Project
items.desc: A free, human-narrated library of 14,000 educational and cultural texts in 22 languages — free to stream, free to download.

items.title: Access Scholars
items.desc: Full scholarships for visually impaired students pursuing STEM and design degrees. 180 scholars funded since 2018.

items.title: Workforce Pathway
items.desc: Employer partnerships that match trained TEWW users to accessibility-mature companies. 1,200+ placements to date.

items.title: Prevention & Screening
items.desc: Mobile clinics deliver vision screenings in rural and underserved areas — 220,000 people screened since 2020.

items.title: Accessible Civic Tech
items.desc: We partner with city and national governments to make public services — ballots, tax filings, benefits — usable for everyone.

items.title: TEWW Translator Guild
items.desc: Our volunteer network of 240+ native speakers localises every interface, guide, and audio file we publish.

featuredStory.pullQuote: For the first time, the internet feels like mine too.
featuredStory.title: Amara Mwangi started her own delivery business this year. She also happens to be blind.
featuredStory.desc: A year ago, Amara had never used a smartphone. Today, she runs a food delivery business in Lagos with 40 regular customers. We spent a week with her learning how TEWW's Screen Reader and Navigation Aid fit into her day — and what the tools still need to become.
featuredStory.author: Maya Osei

volunteers.heroSub: Our work happens because 800+ volunteers around the world give what they can — an hour, an afternoon, a pull request. Here's where to start.

roles.title: Translator
roles.desc: Localise our interfaces, docs, and audio scripts into your language.

roles.title: Open-source contributor
roles.desc: Write and review code for our Screen Reader, Magnifier, and Navigation apps.

roles.title: Audio narrator
roles.desc: Record human voice-overs for our Audiolibrary. Every voice counts.

roles.title: Training facilitator
roles.desc: Lead our 4-week digital literacy bootcamps, in-person or virtual.

roles.title: Accessibility tester
roles.desc: Help test new releases. Guided sessions — no prior testing experience needed.

roles.title: Community organiser
roles.desc: Build a local TEWW chapter. Host events, workshops, and recruitment.

roles.title: Writer & editor
roles.desc: Contribute to our blog, stories, and grant applications.

roles.title: Photographer / videographer
roles.desc: Document field work — stories, events, training days. With consent.

steps.title: Apply
steps.desc: Fill out a short form. It takes 6 minutes. We read every application within 48 hours.

steps.title: Orientation call
steps.desc: A 30-minute video or phone call with a program coordinator to match you to the right role.

steps.title: Onboarding
steps.desc: Self-paced training, plus a live kickoff session with other new volunteers in your region.

steps.title: Meet your mentor
steps.desc: Every volunteer is paired with an experienced mentor for their first three months.

steps.title: Start contributing
steps.desc: First task, first translation, first code review — whatever your role, you're shipping in week two.

about.heroSub: Five honest questions about Third Eye Worldwide — who we are, what we do, what we stand for, and why we think you should come build with us.

faqs.question: Who are we?
faqs.body:
Third Eye Worldwide (TEWW) is a registered non-profit technology NGO, founded in Mumbai in 2025 by two engineers — one sighted, one blind — who believed the tools available to visually impaired users were decades behind what was possible.

Today we're a team of 120 full-time staff and 800+ active volunteers, operating across 47 countries from regional offices in Mumbai, Nairobi, São Paulo, Mexico City, Manila, Cairo, Dakar, and Jakarta. Half of our staff and leadership live with visual impairment — nothing is built about us, without us.

faqs.question: What do we do?
faqs.body:
We build, distribute, and teach assistive technology — all of it free, all of it open source. Our core work falls into four buckets:

  • Software. Screen reader, visual magnifier, navigation aid, and an audio workbench — localised in 41 languages, tuned for 2G networks and five-year-old devices.
  • Hardware. The Device Lending Library has placed 18,000+ pre-configured smartphones with users who can't afford one.
  • Training. Digital literacy workshops for visually impaired individuals and the teachers, parents, and employers around them.
  • Research & advocacy. Field studies, WCAG contributions, and policy work that makes the whole ecosystem better.

faqs.question: Our mission.
faqs.body:
To give every visually impaired person on earth full, independent, dignified access to the digital world — at no cost, in their own language, on whatever device they already own.

We measure this mission in three ways: the number of people who gain a capability they didn't have yesterday, the share of our tools that remain free and open forever, and the percentage of users who say our software feels like it was built for them, not adapted to them.

faqs.question: Our values?
faqs.body:
Five non-negotiables. Every hire, product decision, and partnership gets measured against them:

  • Dignity first. Accessibility isn't a feature we bolt on — it's the foundation we start from.
  • Nothing about us, without us. No product, policy, or program is designed without the people it serves in the room.
  • Open by default. MIT-licensed code, crowd-sourced translations, public research.
  • Built for where people live. If it only works in a San Francisco lab, it doesn't work.
  • Radical transparency. Every dollar is traceable, every impact number auditable, every mistake we make is published.

faqs.question: Why should you join us?
faqs.body:
Because the work is unambiguous, measurable, and personal. Every feature you ship, every language you translate, every device you configure ends up in the hands of a real person who gains independence they didn't have before.

  • You'll have ownership. Small teams, big mandates. Engineers ship to production in week one; program leads run regional strategy.
  • You'll work with experts by experience. Half your colleagues navigate the tools we build every day as users.
  • Your work stays free. Everything you build is MIT-licensed forever. No acquisitions, no paywalls, no "pro tier" someday.
  • You'll get paid fairly. Transparent salary bands, pegged to local cost of living — benchmarked to the 75th percentile of non-profit tech.

team.name: Arjun Rao
team.bio: Blind from birth. Built the first TEWW Screen Reader prototype in 2025. Leads global strategy from Mumbai.

team.name: Priya Sharma
team.bio: Engineer and disability-rights advocate. Oversees all product and engineering across the org.

team.name: Maya Osei
team.bio: Runs field operations across 47 countries. Former director of West Africa Inclusion Network.

team.name: Dr. Rhea Nair
team.bio: Publishes field research on assistive tech adoption. Leads our accessibility audits and WCAG contributions.

team.name: Juma Kimani
team.bio: Oversees 11 country teams across East Africa. Based in Nairobi, speaks six languages.

team.name: Tomás Luján
team.bio: Low-vision from age nine. Leads our 40-person engineering org and open-source stewardship.

team.name: Sofia Ferreira
team.bio: Runs our volunteer network of 800+ contributors and the TEWW Translator Guild.

team.name: Kenji Arata
team.bio: Former mobile carrier exec. Leads device partnerships and subsidised data programs.
